decision_framing.project_description: Integration of a dynamic creative optimization platform to personalize digital ad variants based on real-time user demographic and browsing behavior data.
decision_framing.summary: Novavita Health Tech is evaluating Novavita Dynamic Creative: Integration of a dynamic creative optimization platform to personalize digital ad variants based on real-time user demographic and browsing behavior data. The data enrichment strategy has not been finalized.

plan.clarifying_question: What is your data enrichment strategy?

This is explicitly noted as fabricated in the company docs. (ref: integrated_workflow_performance)

citations.claim: Deeply embedding automated features directly into marketing workflows yields noticeable gains in user acquisition efficiency, customer response rates, and absolute return performance.

explainability.text:
Cost impact  
Driven by real build cost ($180K) and modeled integration ($36K Y1, $48.6K Y2) and model-update ($18K Y1, $27K Y2) costs; inference budget ($8.4K/yr) is a flagged assumption with no benchmark validation. Hosting architecture (first-party only) is also flagged as user-unknown, affecting recurring cost structure. Confidence: 55.

Speed to value  
Payback (4.1–4.2 months) and ROI (3.2–3.3×) derive from ad spend ($150K/mo), AOV ($120), and effective conversion lift (31.5–32%), which clamps the flagged 45% claim (exceeds 32% benchmark ceiling, documented as fabricated). Sanity check flags payback below 6-month realistic minimum, suggesting cost understatement. Confidence: 40.

Process impact  
Additional conversions (4,763–4,838/yr) flow from current conversion rate (2.1%, real) and clamped lift; the 45% claim is explicitly flagged as fabricated versus 20–25% SaaS range. Integration cost ramp (Y1→Y2) reflects assumed process complexity without benchmark grounding. Confidence: 45.

Technology impact  
First-party hosting assumption (flagged: user unknown) drives inference budget ($8.4K) and model-update cadence; the inference-to-build-cost ratio lacks benchmark substantiation. Build cost ($180K) is the only validated technology capital input. Confidence: 50.

Overall confidence is low (≈45) due to fabricated conversion lift, unvalidated operating costs, unknown hosting choice, and sanity-check violations on payback and ROI ceilings.

citations.claim: Deeply embedding automated features directly into marketing workflows yields noticeable gains in user acquisition efficiency, customer response rates, and absolute return performance.

explainability.text:
Cost impact: Driven by initial build cost ($180k), annual inference budget ($8.4k), and modeled integration ($81k Y1/$109k Y2) and model update costs ($32k/$49k). Flagged assumptions: operating budget $8.4k unvalidated; third-party enrichment architecture (unknown) likely inflates integration costs. Confidence: 60.

Speed to value: Payback of 4.8–4.9 months driven by annual value ($571k–$580k) from ad spend ($150k), conversion rate (2.1%), AOV ($120), and effective lift (31.5–32%). Flagged assumptions: claimed 45% lift fabricated; payback below 6-month realistic threshold per sanity check. Confidence: 45.

Process impact: Effective conversion lift of 31.5–32% (additional ~4.8k conversions/yr) derived from current rate (2.1%) and modeled lift, but claimed 45% lift is fabricated and exceeds SaaS-only realistic range (20–25%). Flagged assumption: conversion lift fabricated. Confidence: 40.

Technology impact: Third-party enrichment architecture drives integration and licensing costs; build ($180k) and inference budget ($8.4k) are company-provided. Flagged assumptions: hosting architecture unknown; operating budget unvalidated. Confidence: 55.

Overall confidence is low (~50%) due to fabricated conversion lift, unvalidated operating budget, and unknown hosting architecture affecting all dimensions.

No operating-cost benchmark exists in the provided set to validate this figure. While general cost-structure reasoning could apply, the claim cannot be assessed against any available benchmark standard.

recommendation.reasoning: Scenario A delivers a likely-case ROI of 3.27x versus Scenario B's 1.63x — roughly double the return — while costing $254,000 less over three years ($407K vs. $661K). Both branches share the same core fabricated assumption (45% conversion lift) and unvalidated operating-cost figures, so neither is "clean." However, Scenario A carries one additional output-sanity flag (ROI exceeding the realistic ceiling of 3.2x), and its overall confidence is lower (45% vs. 50%). Despite the extra risk flag, the $254K cost advantage and 2x ROI differential are large enough that Scenario A remains the stronger financial bet — provided the cost estimate is validated before committing, as both branches show signs of potential cost understatement.
recommendation.confidence_caveat: This recommendation carries meaningful risk because both branches operate at low overall confidence (45% for A, 50% for B), and the cost estimates in both cases appear potentially understated relative to the company's operational scale.
